ui.sub: Learn Kikuyu — built on a dictionary and scholarly grammars
ui.home.intro: All content is drawn from reliable sources: the native Gĩkũyũ dictionary and the scholarly grammars (the Sketch Grammar and Gecaga & Kirkaldy-Willis). Your progress is stored locally in your browser.
ui.home.how1: Cards — spaced repetition with the Leitner system, choose a topic
ui.home.how2: Quiz — translate in either direction, or type the answer
ui.cards.title: Cards with spaced repetition
ui.cards.lede: The Leitner system: five boxes. "Knew it" moves the card up (longer interval); "Difficult" sends it back to box 1.
ui.cards.doneSub: No more cards are due in this topic at the moment.
ui.quiz.title: Quiz
ui.quiz.lede: Drawn from the dictionary. Choose a mode, answer ten questions, see your result.
ui.quiz.resMid: Well done — review the tricky ones.
ui.quiz.resLow: Do a round of cards and try again.
ui.grammar.title: Grammar
ui.grammar.lede: Bantu structure, SVO. Based on the Sketch Grammar (Rice University) and Gecaga & Kirkaldy-Willis (1953).
ui.sentences.title: Example sentences
ui.sentences.lede: Real, glossed sentences from the Sketch Grammar. Tap a sentence to reveal its translation.
ui.sentences.note: Time markers such as "(today)" and "(yesterday)" show which of the seven tenses is in use — useful reading practice.
ui.dict.title: Dictionary
ui.dict.lede: The full Gĩkũyũ–English dictionary (Wambũi M / Gĩkũyũ and Mũmbi Women, 2022).

sentences.copula6: It is I who am a teacher.
sentences.action_past3: The little dog stole a chicken (today).
sentences.action_past4: The man will steal a chicken (today).
sentences.action_past5: The teachers will read the books (today).
sentences.action_past6: The maize grew (the day before yesterday).
sentences.action_past7: The woman returned home with the children.
sentences.action_past8: And then he climbed a ladder.

grammar.h:
The grammar rests on two scholarly sources and is cross-checked against the dictionary: A Basic Sketch Grammar of Gĩkũyũ (Englebretson et al., Rice University) for modern, precise analysis, and A Short Kikuyu Grammar (Gecaga & Kirkaldy-Willis, 1953) for practical paradigms and greetings.

Abbreviations: SP = subject prefix · T = tense · OP = object prefix · FV = final vowel · NC = noun class.

Gĩkũyũ is written with 18 letters: a, b, c, e, g, h, i, ĩ, k, m, n, o, r, t, u, ũ, w, y. The letters d, f, j, l, p, q, s, v, x, z do not occur.

7 vowels, each with a single sound: a, e, i, ĩ, o, u, ũ. ĩ and ũ are distinct, closer vowels — not i/u with an accent.

SpellingPronunciation
clike "ch" in "church"
thlike "th" in "this" (fricative)
nylike Spanish ñ
ng'like "ng" in "sing"
gvoiced, softer between vowels

Gĩkũyũ is tonal: pitch distinguishes words and grammatical forms. Tones are not marked in writing, but they are essential in speech.

Like all Bantu languages, Gĩkũyũ sorts nouns into classes, marked by prefixes. Singular and plural belong to different classes, and the class governs agreement (concord) on verbs, adjectives, numerals and pronouns.

Cl.PrefixExampleMeaning
1mũ-mũndũperson
2a-andũpeople
3mũ-mũtĩtree
4mĩ-mĩtĩtrees
5rĩ-/i-ihũaflower
6ma-mahũaflowers
7kĩ-/gĩ-kĩrĩmamountain
8i-/ci-irĩmamountains
9N-mbataduck
10N-mbataducks
11rũ-rũĩgĩeagle
12ka-/ga-kanuamouth (dim.)
13tũ-tũnuamouths (dim.)
14ũ-ũcũrũporridge (abstract)
15kũ-/gũ-gũtũear / infinitive
16ha-handũplace (definite)
17kũ-/gũ-kũndũplace (indefinite)

The infinitive (class 15, gũ-/kũ-) is itself a noun class — hence "gũthoma" = "the act of reading".

Everything that refers to a noun aligns with its class. Compare classes 3 and 4:

KikuyuMeaning
mũtĩ ũyũ nĩ mũnenethis tree is big (cl. 3)
mĩtĩ ĩno nĩ mĩnenethese trees are big (cl. 4)

The subject prefix (SC) on the verb also follows the class:

Cl.123478910
SCa-ma-ũ-ĩ-kĩ-i-/ci-ĩ-ci-

A verb is assembled from slots around a stem (Sketch Grammar, table 18):

FOC – SP – NEG – T – OP – STEM – (derivation) – ASP – FV

FOC = focus (nĩ-) · SP = subject · NEG = negation · T = tense · OP = object · ASP = aspect · FV = final vowel.

Example: nĩ-a-mũ-end-ĩr-i-e → nĩamwendeirie "he/she sold him/her something".

PersonPrefix
IN- (→ nd-/nj-/n-)
wetũ-
you (sg.)ũ-
you (pl.)mũ-
he/she (cl. 1)a-
they (cl. 2)ma-

The first person singular is the nasal N-, which changes shape according to the following sound (nd-, nj-, n-, ndĩ-). "He/she" is really class 1 agreement (a-), not a separate "3SG" pronoun.

Gĩkũyũ has seven tenses, marked by a prefix between the subject and the stem. Tone often carries the decisive distinction (Sketch Grammar, table 21):

TensePrefix
Remote pasta-
Near pastra- / raa-
Current pasta- / ∅
Present∅ / ra-
Near futureka-
Futurerĩĩ-
Remote futurekaa-

Example with gũũka "to come":
nĩnjũkaga — I come (habitually)
nĩndĩroka — I am coming (right now)

The same prefix can cover several tenses, and the same tense can take several prefixes. Tense must be read together with aspect and tone.

SuffixFunction
-ag-habitual / repeated
-ir-/-ĩr-completive
-athe usual final vowel
-efinal vowel in, among others, the completive/subjunctive

Main clauses are negated with ti- after the subject. When the subject is a single vowel (such as cl. 1 a-), the negation comes first as nd-.

KikuyuMeaning
ndũthiagayou do not go (habitually)
ndarokahe/she is not coming
mbembe ti nyamũmaize is not an animal

In relative clauses the negative prefix ta- is used instead — never ti-.

grammar.t: The focus particle nĩ- & the copula
grammar.h:
nĩ- comes first on the verb and raises its pragmatic status (focus). The same nĩ serves as the copula "is":

KikuyuMeaning
ngui nĩ nyamũa dog is an animal
kĩrĩma nĩ kĩnenethe mountain is big
nĩ niĩit is me

The negative copula is ti: mbembe ti nyamũ "maize is not an animal".

Practical forms from Gecaga & Kirkaldy-Willis (1953):

KikuyuMeaning
Wĩ mwega?Are you well? (to one person)
Mũrĩ ega?Are you well? (to several)
Waigua atĩa?How are you?
Ĩĩ, ndĩ mwegaYes, I am well
Thiĩ na wegaGoodbye (go in peace)
HĩtũkaCome in

Days of the week (mũthenya wa …): kiumia (Sun), mwambĩrĩrio (Mon), waĩrĩ/wakerĩ (Tue), wetatũ/wagatatũ (Wed), wena (Thu), wetano/wagatano (Fri), njuma (Sat). The names vary by region.
